questions.question: The Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme was introduced to:
options: Incentivize companies to increase domestic manufacturing — financial incentives based on incremental sales; initially 13 sectors including mobile phones, pharmaceuticals, automobiles, textiles
Replace GST
Provide employment guarantee
Support agriculture
questions.explanation: PLI Scheme (2020-21): government gives manufacturers financial incentives (3-5% of incremental production over a base year) to boost domestic manufacturing. Initially 13 sectors: mobile phones (Apple, Samsung set up factories), pharmaceuticals (API), automobiles, textiles, food processing, etc. Aims to make India a global manufacturing hub.

questions.question: The Make in India initiative was launched by:
options: PM Narendra Modi in September 2014 — to make India a global manufacturing hub; focuses on 25 sectors; lion (Ashoka Chakra wheel as wheels) is its symbol
PM Manmohan Singh
NITI Aayog independently
Ministry of Commerce
questions.explanation: Make in India (September 25, 2014): India as global design and manufacturing hub. 25 focus sectors: automobiles, aviation, chemicals, IT, pharma, construction, food processing, textiles, etc. The iconic logo: walking lion made of cogwheels (industry symbol). Complemented by: Invest India, Startup India, Skill India, Digital India.

questions.question: India's largest public sector undertaking (PSU) by revenue is:
options: Indian Oil Corporation (IOC) — petroleum refining and marketing; consistently India's largest company by revenue; followed by ONGC, BPCL
Life Insurance Corporation (LIC)
State Bank of India
NTPC
questions.explanation: Indian Oil Corporation (IOC): India's largest company by revenue (~Rs 8 lakh crore). It is a Fortune 500 company (ranked ~94th globally). Refines and markets petroleum products through its extensive pipeline and retail network (~30,000 outlets). Other large PSUs: ONGC (oil exploration), BPCL, HPCL, Coal India, SBI, LIC, NTPC.

questions.question: The phenomenon of 'Dutch Disease' refers to:
options: Boom in one sector (e.g., natural resource discovery) causing currency appreciation and decline in other export sectors (especially manufacturing) — named after Netherlands' natural gas boom in 1960s
A type of financial crisis
An agricultural disease
Import of cheap manufactured goods
questions.explanation: Dutch Disease: named after Netherlands' North Sea gas discovery (1959). Large foreign currency inflows from resource boom → currency appreciates → manufacturing becomes uncompetitive → deindustrialization. Examples: Nigeria (oil), Venezuela (oil). Prevention: sovereign wealth funds (Norway), diversification strategies.

questions.question: 'Disinvestment' in Indian public sector means:
options: Government selling its equity stake in PSUs (Public Sector Undertakings) — partial disinvestment: retains majority; strategic disinvestment: transfers management control; 'privatization' used when full control is transferred
Nationalization
Government investing more in PSUs
PSU borrowing funds
questions.explanation: Disinvestment: government sells equity shares in PSUs to raise revenue and improve PSU efficiency. Types: Minority disinvestment (retains >51%, no control change), Majority disinvestment (retains <51%), Strategic Disinvestment (control transferred — like Air India sale to Tata 2022). DIPAM (Dept of Investment and Public Asset Management) manages disinvestment. Annual target: Rs 50,000-60,000 crore.

questions.question: The 'Economic corridor' concept in India refers to:
options: Integrated industrial and logistics infrastructure along transportation routes — Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor (DMIC), Chennai-Bengaluru (CBIC), Amritsar-Kolkata (AKIC), Bangalore-Mumbai (BMIC)
Only road corridors
Trade routes with neighboring countries
Exclusive economic zones at sea
questions.explanation: Industrial Corridors: Delhi-Mumbai IC (DMIC, flagship: 1483 km along DFC — Dedicated Freight Corridor, 24 manufacturing nodes, $90 billion investment plan). Chennai-Bengaluru IC (CBIC). Amritsar-Kolkata IC (AKIC). Hyderabad-Nagpur, Hyderabad-Warangal IC. Funded by: National Industrial Corridor Development Corporation (NICDC).

questions.question: Pradhan Mantri Bhartiya Janaushadhi Pariyojana (PMBJP) provides:
options: Quality generic medicines at affordable prices through Jan Aushadhi Kendras (pharmacies) — launched 2008, expanded 2015; ~10,000+ Kendras in 2023; medicines priced 50-90% lower than branded drugs
Free medicines everywhere
Only vaccines
Only surgical equipment
questions.explanation: PMBJP (Bureau of Pharma PSUs of India — BPPI): network of Jan Aushadhi Kendras selling 1,900+ generic medicines and 300+ surgical items at prices 50-90% below branded alternatives. Ensures quality through testing (ISP and NABL-accredited labs). Benefited: affordable medicines for common diseases (diabetes, hypertension, cardiac, cancer, TB). Also: SuJaK (Sugam Jan Aushadi Kendras) for remote areas.

questions.question: The concept of 'infant industry protection' argues:
options: New/young industries in developing countries need temporary tariff protection to grow and become internationally competitive — used by USA (19th century), Germany (Bismarck era), Japan, South Korea, and now by developing economies including India
All industries need permanent protection
Free trade is never beneficial
Only agricultural industries need protection
questions.explanation: Infant Industry Argument (Friedrich List, 1841 — 'National System of Political Economy'): nascent industries with potential scale economies need temporary protection (tariffs, subsidies) against established foreign competitors until they achieve global competitiveness. USA protected its manufacturing (1800s-1900s) with high tariffs. South Korea's POSCO steel. Counter: protection tends to become permanent, breeds inefficiency.

questions.question: The National Credit Guarantee Trust (NCGTC) oversees:
options: Credit guarantees for MSME loans (without collateral) — provides guarantee cover to lending institutions so they lend to MSMEs; also operates PM Mudra Yojana and PM SVANidhi guarantees
Housing loan guarantees
Agricultural loan waivers
Export credit guarantees (that's ECGC)
questions.explanation: NCGTC (National Credit Guarantee Trustee Company, 2014): provides credit guarantees for MSME loans through: Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises (CGTMSE), Credit Guarantee Fund for Skill Development (CGFSD), Credit Guarantee Fund for Educational Loans (CGFEL), Credit Guarantee Scheme for Start-Ups. Borrowers don't need collateral; banks get guaranteed coverage.

questions.question: India's 'National Mission on Transformative Mobility and Battery Storage' (NMTMBS) aims at:
options: Accelerating adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) — policies for EV manufacturing, charging infrastructure, battery recycling; FAME-II scheme for EV subsidies; targets: 30% EV penetration by 2030 for private cars
Petroleum refining
Public transport only
Two-wheelers only
questions.explanation: EV Mission: FAME India (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles): Phase-I (2015-19), Phase-II (2019-24, Rs 10,000 crore): subsidies for EV purchase, charging infrastructure. PLI for Advanced Chemistry Cell batteries (ACC-PLI, Rs 18,100 crore). NMTMBS: inter-ministerial mission for EV ecosystem. India wants 30% EV by 2030 for all categories; 100% EV for 2-wheelers and 3-wheelers.

questions.question: Structural unemployment is caused by:
options: Economic downturns reducing demand
Seasonal variations in production
Mismatch between workers' skills and available jobs due to technological change
Workers voluntarily changing jobs
questions.explanation: Structural unemployment: skills mismatch due to technology changes, industry shifts, or globalization. Long-term; requires retraining. E.g., manufacturing workers displaced by automation.

questions.question: The Bombay Plan (1944) was drafted by Indian industrialists including Tata and Birla, proposing:
options: Free market economy with no government intervention
Central planning with significant state investment in industry
Complete nationalization of all industries
Agricultural-led growth model
questions.explanation: Bombay Plan: 8 industrialists (JRD Tata, GD Birla, etc.) proposed a 15-year plan with heavy state investment in infrastructure and industry, import substitution, and mixed economy framework.

questions.question: The MSME sector in India is defined under the MSME Development (Amendment) Act 2020 based on:
options: Number of employees only
Investment in plant & machinery/equipment AND annual turnover
Annual turnover only
Capital employed only
questions.explanation: MSME 2020 definition: Micro (Investment ≤Rs 1 cr, Turnover ≤Rs 5 cr), Small (Investment ≤Rs 10 cr, Turnover ≤Rs 50 cr), Medium (Investment ≤Rs 50 cr, Turnover ≤Rs 250 cr). Composite criteria (investment + turnover).

questions.question: The Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT) is responsible for:
options: Collecting import/export duties
Formulating and implementing India's Foreign Trade Policy (FTP)
Regulating foreign exchange transactions
Negotiating trade agreements
questions.explanation: DGFT: under Ministry of Commerce and Industry. Implements FTP, issues Importer Exporter Code (IEC), administers export promotion schemes (EPCG, Advance Authorization, MEIS/RODTEP). Also issues export/import licenses.

questions.question: The 'Smile Curve' concept in international trade (coined by Stan Shih of Acer) shows that:
options: Profits are highest in the middle of the value chain (manufacturing)
Profits are highest at both ends of the value chain (R&D/design and marketing/services), not in manufacturing
Developing countries benefit most from trade
Small economies grow faster
questions.explanation: Smile Curve: value added is highest at R&D/design (upstream) and brand/marketing/services (downstream) ends; manufacturing in the middle captures less value. Explains why Apple earns more than Foxconn despite Foxconn making iPhones.

questions.question: India's National Monetization Pipeline (NMP) aims to monetize government/PSU brownfield assets worth approximately:
options: Rs 1 lakh crore
Rs 3 lakh crore
Rs 6 lakh crore
Rs 10 lakh crore
questions.explanation: NMP (August 2021): Rs 6 lakh crore worth of brownfield infrastructure assets to be monetized over 4 years (FY2022-25). Assets: NHAI roads, railway stations, airports, power lines, gas pipelines, telecom towers. NITI Aayog designed it.

questions.question: Privatization means:
options: Transfer of ownership or management of state-owned enterprises to private sector — through disinvestment (selling government equity), strategic sale, or outright sale
Government taking over private companies
Increase in government spending
Tax reduction only
questions.explanation: Privatization: government ownership/control transferred to private sector. Disinvestment: government sells part of its equity in PSUs (Public Sector Undertakings). Strategic sale: transfer of management control + equity. Full privatization: 100% sale. India's disinvestment target: Rs 65,000 crore (2023-24). Air India sold to Tata Sons (2022) is a major strategic disinvestment.

questions.question: The concept of 'Comparative Advantage' in international trade was developed by:
options: David Ricardo (1817, 'On the Principles of Political Economy and Taxation') — countries should specialize in producing goods they produce relatively more efficiently, even if less efficient absolutely
Adam Smith (Absolute Advantage)
John Keynes
Paul Krugman (New Trade Theory)
questions.explanation: Comparative Advantage (Ricardo): a country should specialize in goods where it has the lowest opportunity cost relative to other goods, not necessarily the lowest absolute cost. Even if one country is more efficient in all goods, both benefit from trade through specialization. Basis for international free trade theory. Example: India in IT services, China in manufacturing.

questions.question: The Delhi-Mumbai Industrial Corridor (DMIC) is a joint initiative with which country?
options: USA
China
Japan
Germany
questions.explanation: DMIC: India-Japan joint project along the 1,483-km Western Dedicated Freight Corridor. 24 manufacturing cities/nodes planned across 6 states. Japan invested in DMIC Development Corporation.

questions.question: The Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme provides incentive as a percentage of:
options: Fixed capital investment
Incremental sales over a base year
Employment generated
Export value
questions.explanation: PLI: companies receive incentive (4-20% depending on sector) on incremental sales above their base year (2019-20). Covers 14 sectors. Aims to attract investment, enhance manufacturing, and boost exports.

questions.question: Who is known as the 'Father of Indian Economic Planning'?
options: Jawaharlal Nehru
P.C. Mahalanobis
Manmohan Singh
V.K.R.V. Rao
questions.explanation: P.C. Mahalanobis (1893-1972): statistician and planner, architect of India's second Five Year Plan (1956-61). Founder of ISI (Indian Statistical Institute). His 'two-sector' model emphasized heavy industry.

questions.question: The term 'Dutch Disease' in economics refers to:
options: Economic downturn caused by flood/natural disaster
Decline in manufacturing sector caused by a large discovery/boom in natural resources (e.g., oil)
High inflation in a welfare state
Economic recession caused by high wages
questions.explanation: Dutch Disease: named after Netherlands' 1960s natural gas discovery. Resource boom → currency appreciates → exports of other sectors (manufacturing) become uncompetitive → deindustrialization. Affects many resource-rich nations.

questions.question: Under the revised MSME definition, which of the following is the primary criterion used to classify an enterprise as a 'Micro' enterprise?
options: Only the total number of employees
Combination of investment in plant and machinery and annual turnover
Only the annual turnover of the enterprise
Investment in fixed assets regardless of turnover
questions.explanation: The revised definition uses a composite criterion of both investment and annual turnover to classify Micro, Small, and Medium enterprises.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'TReDS' (Trade Receivables Discounting System) platform in the context of MSMEs?
options: A portal for registering new MSME units
A mechanism for providing equity funding to startups
An online platform to facilitate the discounting of trade receivables to solve liquidity issues
A credit rating agency for small-scale industrial units
questions.explanation: TReDS is an institutional mechanism to address the problem of delayed payments to MSMEs by discounting their invoices.

questions.question: How does an increase in the 'Cash Reserve Ratio' (CRR) by the RBI typically impact the operational capacity of MSMEs?
options: It increases the availability of working capital loans
It reduces the cost of borrowing for small industries
It may lead to a liquidity crunch by reducing the lendable resources of banks
It has no impact as MSMEs do not rely on commercial bank credit
questions.explanation: Higher CRR reduces the loanable funds available with banks, potentially tightening credit flow to credit-dependent MSMEs.

questions.question: The 'Champions' portal' launched by the Government of India is primarily aimed at which of the following objectives?
options: Providing direct subsidies for electricity consumption
Grievance redressal and facilitating the growth of MSMEs into larger enterprises
Regulating the import of raw materials for small industries
Managing the payroll of employees in the informal sector
questions.explanation: The Champions portal focuses on grievance redressal and helping MSMEs scale up to become 'Champions'.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key characteristic of 'Udyam Registration' for MSMEs in India?
options: It is a manual process requiring physical verification of the factory
It is a permanent registration based on self-declaration via an online portal
It requires a minimum paid-up capital of 10 lakh rupees
It is only applicable to enterprises located in Special Economic Zones
questions.explanation: Udyam Registration is a fully online, paperless, and self-declaration-based permanent registration process.

questions.question: In the context of the Indian economy, what is the most likely impact of a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee on MSMEs primarily focused on export-oriented manufacturing?
options: It makes their exports more expensive in the global market
It increases the cost of domestic labor, reducing competitiveness
It generally makes their products more price-competitive in international markets
It leads to an automatic increase in the cost of domestic raw materials
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee makes Indian exports cheaper for foreign buyers, increasing the competitiveness of export-oriented MSMEs.

questions.question: Which of the following is the primary objective of the 'Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises' (CGTMSE)?
options: To provide direct grants to MSMEs for technology upgrades
To provide collateral-free credit to micro and small enterprises
To act as a regulatory body for MSME pricing
To provide insurance against fire and natural disasters
questions.explanation: CGTMSE provides credit guarantees to lenders to encourage them to provide collateral-free loans to MSMEs.

questions.question: The concept of 'Cluster Development' in the MSME sector is intended to achieve which of the following?
options: The concentration of all MSMEs in a single metropolitan city
Economies of scale through shared infrastructure and collective bargaining
The elimination of competition between small-scale units
The conversion of all MSMEs into Public Sector Undertakings
questions.explanation: Cluster development allows MSMEs to share common facility centers and reduce costs through collective resource utilization.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding the 'Priority Sector Lending' (PSL) norms is correct in relation to MSMEs?
options: Banks are not required to lend to MSMEs under PSL
MSME lending is a component of PSL, ensuring targeted credit flow to the sector
PSL norms only apply to the agricultural sector, not the industrial sector
PSL mandates that all MSME loans must be interest-free
questions.explanation: PSL mandates that a specific percentage of bank lending must go to sectors like agriculture and MSMEs to ensure inclusive growth.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the 'ZED' (Zero Defect Zero Effect) scheme for MSMEs?
options: To ensure zero employees are laid off during economic downturns
To encourage quality manufacturing (Zero Defect) and environmental sustainability (Zero Effect)
To eliminate all taxes for small-scale industries
To ensure zero dependence on imported machinery
questions.explanation: ZED aims to improve quality standards to reduce waste and minimize the environmental footprint of MSME manufacturing.

questions.question: Which monetary policy tool involves the central bank injecting liquidity into the economy by purchasing government securities?
options: Open Market Operations
Bank Rate Policy
Cash Reserve Ratio Adjustment
Statutory Liquidity Ratio Adjustment
questions.explanation: Open Market Operations (OMO) involve buying/selling government securities to regulate liquidity. Purchasing securities injects liquidity into the banking system.

questions.question: Which of the following is a direct fiscal indicator of India's economic health?
options: GDP Growth Rate
Fiscal Deficit
Current Account Deficit
Inflation Rate
questions.explanation: Fiscal Deficit measures the gap between government revenue and expenditure, excluding non-debt expenditure. It directly reflects fiscal policy sustainability.

questions.question: Which sectoral policy has the greatest impact on the competitiveness of MSMEs?
options: Trade Liberalization
Monetary Tightening
Interest Rate Cuts
Corporate Tax Reforms
questions.explanation: Trade liberalization reduces import competition and enhances export opportunities for MSMEs by lowering barriers to international trade.

questions.question: A depreciation of the Indian rupee against the US dollar is most likely to
options: Increase exports
Decrease imports
Worsen inflation
Improve trade balance
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation raises import costs, contributing to inflation. While exports may rise, the immediate impact is often higher inflationary pressure.

questions.question: Which of the following is a component of India's balance of payments?
options: FDI Inflows
Capital Account
Statutory Liquidity Ratio
Fiscal Deficit
questions.explanation: Capital Account records capital transfers and acquisition/disposal of non-financial assets. It is a key component of balance of payments.

questions.question: Which of the following is a demand-pull inflationary factor?
options: Cost-push inflation
Monopoly power
High taxation
Excess demand
questions.explanation: Demand-pull inflation occurs when aggregate demand exceeds aggregate supply, leading to rising prices due to excess demand.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator reflects the government's ability to meet short-term obligations?
options: Gross Domestic Product
Primary Deficit
Tax Revenue
Monetization of Debt
questions.explanation: Primary Deficit (excluding interest payments) indicates the government's capacity to fund current expenditures without relying on past borrowing.

questions.question: Which of the following is a structural reform aimed at improving India's trade balance?
options: Export Promotion Councils
Import Duties
Domestic Subsidies
Monetary Tightening
questions.explanation: Export Promotion Councils support MSMEs and industries to boost exports, directly improving the trade balance.

questions.question: Which of the following is a consequence of sterilized open market operations?
options: Increased money supply
Reduced inflation
Increased interest rates
No change in money supply
questions.explanation: Sterilized OMO involves offsetting liquidity injections through other measures, ensuring no net change in money supply.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key factor in the rising non-performing assets (NPAs) in the Indian banking sector?
options: Over-leveraged MSMEs
Weak inflation targeting
High fiscal deficit
Currency depreciation
questions.explanation: Over-leveraged MSMEs default on loans due to high debt levels, contributing to NPAs in commercial banks.

questions.question: Which of the following is a direct impact of a rise in the Bank Rate?
options: Increased credit availability
Lower inflation
Higher borrowing costs
Increased money supply
questions.explanation: An increase in Bank Rate raises the cost of borrowing for banks, leading to higher interest rates for consumers and businesses.

questions.question: With reference to the revised definition of MSMEs in India, which of the following is the primary basis for the new classification?
options: Investment in plant and machinery only
Annual turnover only
Composite criteria of investment and annual turnover
Number of employees and paid-up capital
questions.explanation: The 2020 revision moved from investment-only to a composite criterion of both investment and annual turnover to encourage growth without losing benefits.

questions.question: How does the 'Udyam Registration' portal primarily assist in the formalization of the MSME sector in India?
options: By providing mandatory equity funding to all registered units
By creating a permanent digital identity for seamless access to government schemes
By exempting all registered MSMEs from GST compliance
By automating the export-import licensing process
questions.explanation: Udyam provides a permanent registration number, facilitating easier access to priority sector lending and government support.

questions.question: In the context of Indian industrial policy, what is the primary objective of the 'Champions' project' launched by the Ministry of MSME?
options: To provide direct cash grants to all micro-enterprises
To transform quality MSMEs into 'Champions' for global competitiveness
To replace the existing MSME classification system
To nationalize small-scale industries for better coordination
questions.explanation: The project aims to upgrade MSMEs through technology and innovation to make them globally competitive.

questions.question: Which of the following is a likely impact of an increase in the 'Cash Reserve Ratio' (CRR) on the MSME sector?
options: Increased availability of working capital loans
Reduced cost of borrowing for small industries
Potential tightening of credit availability due to reduced loanable funds
Automatic increase in the turnover of micro-enterprises
questions.explanation: Higher CRR reduces the liquidity available with banks, which often leads to tighter credit conditions for credit-dependent MSMEs.

questions.question: Consider the 'Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises' (CGTMSE). What is its fundamental purpose?
options: To provide direct loans to entrepreneurs without interest
To provide collateral-free credit by offering guarantees to lenders
To manage the equity shares of small-scale industries
To regulate the interest rates charged by NBFCs
questions.explanation: CGTMSE reduces the risk for banks, encouraging them to lend to MSMEs who lack sufficient collateral.

questions.question: How does a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee typically affect the MSME sector focused on exports?
options: Makes exports more expensive and less competitive
Increases the cost of raw materials for purely domestic producers
Makes Indian exports more price-competitive in the global market
Decreases the total value of foreign exchange earnings
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee makes Indian goods cheaper for foreign buyers, thereby boosting export competitiveness.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding 'Priority Sector Lending' (PSL) is correct in the context of MSMEs?
options: Banks are not required to lend to MSMEs under PSL
MSME lending is a mandatory target for scheduled commercial banks
PSL only applies to agricultural loans, not industrial loans
Only government-owned banks are bound by PSL norms
questions.explanation: MSMEs are a key component of Priority Sector Lending, ensuring they receive a mandated share of bank credit.

questions.question: The 'Cluster Development Programme' for MSMEs is primarily designed to achieve which of the following?
options: To concentrate all MSMEs in a single city to reduce transport costs
To achieve economies of scale through shared infrastructure and services
To eliminate competition between small-scale units
To convert all micro-enterprises into large-scale corporations
questions.explanation: Clusters allow small units to share common facility centers, reducing overheads and increasing efficiency.

questions.question: What is the primary conceptual link between 'Inflation' and the operational costs of MSMEs?
options: Inflation always leads to lower raw material costs
Inflation increases input costs, squeezing profit margins if prices cannot be passed on
Inflation eliminates the need for working capital loans
Inflation leads to a decrease in the demand for MSME products
questions.explanation: Rising input costs (cost-push inflation) hurt MSMEs more as they lack the bargaining power to pass costs to consumers.

questions.question: With reference to the revised definition of MSMEs in India, which of the following is the primary criterion used to categorize an enterprise as 'Micro, Small, or Medium'?
options: Only the annual turnover of the enterprise
Only the total investment in plant and machinery
A composite criterion of investment and annual turnover
The total number of employees employed by the unit
questions.explanation: The 2020 revision shifted from investment-only to a composite criterion involving both investment and annual turnover.

questions.question: How does a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee generally impact the MSME sector in India?
options: It hurts export-oriented MSMEs by making their goods costlier
It benefits import-dependent MSMEs by reducing raw material costs
It benefits export-oriented MSMEs by making their products more competitive globally
It has no impact as MSMEs primarily serve the domestic market
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes Indian exports cheaper for foreign buyers, boosting competitiveness for export-oriented MSMEs.

questions.question: The 'Udyam Registration' portal is primarily designed to achieve which of the following objectives for the MSME sector?
options: To provide direct equity funding to all registered units
To formalize the sector and facilitate access to priority sector lending
To eliminate the need for GST registration for small businesses
To provide permanent tax immunity to micro-enterprises
questions.explanation: Udyam registration formalizes units, allowing them to access government schemes and priority sector credit from banks.

questions.question: In the context of the Indian economy, what is the primary objective of the 'Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises' (CGTMSE)?
options: To provide grants for the purchase of machinery
To provide collateral-free credit to micro and small enterprises
To regulate the interest rates charged by NBFCs
To provide insurance against fire and natural calamities
questions.explanation: CGTMSE provides credit guarantees to lenders to encourage collateral-free lending to MSMEs.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best explains the concept of 'Cluster Development' in the MSME sector?
options: Concentrating all MSMEs in one single city to reduce urban sprawl
Grouping geographically concentrated units of similar industries to achieve economies of scale
Merging small firms into one large corporation to increase market share
Providing subsidies only to units located in rural clusters
questions.explanation: Clusters allow small units to share common infrastructure and resources, reducing costs and increasing efficiency.

questions.question: What is the likely impact of an increase in the Repo Rate by the RBI on the MSME sector?
options: It lowers the cost of borrowing, leading to increased capital expenditure
It increases the cost of working capital loans, potentially squeezing profit margins
It leads to an immediate increase in the export volume of MSMEs
It eliminates the need for MSMEs to seek loans from NBFCs
questions.explanation: Higher Repo rates increase the cost of borrowing for banks, which is passed on to MSMEs as higher loan interest.

questions.question: The 'Champion' portal launched by the Ministry of MSME is specifically aimed at which of the following?
options: Providing free electricity to all micro-enterprises
Grievance redressal and facilitating the growth of MSMEs into 'Champions'
Managing the import of raw materials from ASEAN countries
Calculating the GDP contribution of the industrial sector
questions.explanation: The CHAMPIONS portal is a technology-driven control room for grievance redressal and handholding MSMEs.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key challenge that leads to the 'Missing Middle' phenomenon in the Indian industrial structure?
options: Excessive number of medium-sized enterprises
Lack of transition from micro-enterprises to medium-sized enterprises
Over-reliance on large-scale corporate houses for all production
Complete absence of micro-enterprises in the rural economy
questions.explanation: The 'Missing Middle' refers to the gap where firms stay small to avoid regulatory burdens rather than scaling up.

questions.question: Under the Public Procurement Policy, what is the mandatory percentage of procurement that Central Government Ministries/Departments must make from MSMEs?
questions.explanation: The government mandates a 25% procurement target from MSMEs to support their growth.

questions.question: Under the revised MSME classification, which of the following criteria is used to define a 'Small' enterprise?
options: Investment up to ₹1 crore and turnover up to ₹5 crore
Investment up to ₹10 crore and turnover up to ₹50 crore
Investment up to ₹20 crore and turnover up to ₹100 crore
Investment up to ₹50 crore and turnover up to ₹250 crore
questions.explanation: The revised 2020 criteria use a composite criterion of investment and turnover; for Small enterprises, it is ₹10 cr investment and ₹50 cr turnover.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the primary objective of the 'TReDS' (Trade Receivables Discounting System) platform for MSMEs?
options: To provide long-term equity funding for expansion
To facilitate the registration of MSMEs under Udyam portal
To resolve the issue of delayed payments by discounting invoices
To provide subsidized electricity and land for industrial clusters
questions.explanation: TReDS is an institutional mechanism to facilitate the discounting of trade receivables to solve liquidity crunch caused by delayed payments.

questions.question: How does a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee typically impact the competitiveness of MSME export-oriented units?
options: It makes exports more expensive and less competitive in global markets
It makes Indian exports cheaper for foreign buyers, potentially increasing demand
It has no impact as MSMEs operate only in the domestic market
It increases the cost of raw materials, which completely offsets any export gain
questions.explanation: Currency depreciation makes domestic goods cheaper in foreign currency terms, enhancing export competitiveness.

questions.question: In the context of the 'Champion' scheme for MSMEs, what is the primary focus of the 'Champions with Champions' initiative?
options: Providing direct grants for purchasing machinery
Linking MSMEs with global value chains through mentorship from large industries
Reducing the GST burden for micro-enterprises
Providing collateral-free loans through the Priority Sector Lending
questions.explanation: The initiative aims to integrate MSMEs into global value chains by leveraging the expertise of larger, successful industries.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Priority Sector Lending' (PSL) as it relates to the MSME sector?
options: Banks are prohibited from charging interest on MSME loans
Banks must allocate a specific percentage of Adjusted Net Bank Credit to MSMEs
Only public sector banks are required to follow PSL norms for MSMEs
PSL requires all MSME loans to be fully guaranteed by the Central Government
questions.explanation: RBI mandates that banks allocate a specific portion of their lending to priority sectors, including MSMEs, to ensure credit flow.

questions.question: The 'Udyam Registration' portal is designed to achieve which of the following goals?
options: To replace the need for GST registration for all small businesses
To create a permanent identification number for MSMEs to access government benefits
To automate the process of filing annual income tax returns
To provide a platform for direct B2C sales of MSME products
questions.explanation: Udyam registration provides a permanent identity and simplifies the process of accessing government schemes and credit.

questions.question: Which of the following is the primary purpose of the 'Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises' (CGTMSE)?
options: To provide direct subsidies for labor costs
To provide collateral-free credit to MSMEs by sharing the risk with lenders
To act as a regulatory body for MSME pricing
To provide insurance against fire and natural calamities for factories

questions.question: Which of the following factors most significantly contributes to the 'Missing Middle' phenomenon in the Indian industrial structure?
options: Over-abundance of large-scale enterprises
The tendency of small firms to stay small to avoid regulatory compliance and taxes
A total absence of micro-enterprises in rural areas
Excessive government subsidies for medium-sized enterprises
questions.explanation: The 'Missing Middle' refers to the lack of medium-sized firms as small firms avoid scaling up to avoid stricter regulatory burdens.

questions.question: What is the intended effect of 'Production Linked Incentive' (PLI) schemes on the MSME sector?
options: To discourage imports by imposing high tariffs on all raw materials
To incentivize domestic manufacturing based on incremental sales of goods
To provide fixed monthly salaries to employees of MSMEs
To eliminate the need for private investment in industrial parks
questions.explanation: PLI schemes provide financial incentives based on the increase in production/sales to boost domestic manufacturing.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of 'Cluster Development' in the MSME sector?
options: Forcing all MSMEs to merge into a single large corporate entity
Concentrating similar industries in one area to share common infrastructure and resources
Restricting MSMEs to operate only within specific geographic boundaries
Eliminating competition between small firms in the same industry
questions.explanation: Cluster development aims to create synergies and reduce costs through shared common facility centers and infrastructure.

questions.question: With reference to the revised definition of MSMEs in India, which of the following is the primary basis for classification?
options: Investment in plant and machinery only
Annual turnover only
Composite criteria of investment and annual turnover
Number of employees and annual export value
questions.explanation: The 2020 revision moved from investment-only to a composite criterion involving both investment and annual turnover.

questions.question: How does an increase in the 'Cash Reserve Ratio' (CRR) by the RBI typically impact the working capital of MSMEs?
options: Increases liquidity, lowering the cost of short-term borrowing
Decreases bankable funds, potentially tightening credit availability
Has no impact as MSMEs rely solely on venture capital
Increases the loan-to-value ratio for collateral-based loans

questions.question: Which of the following is the most likely impact of a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee on the MSME export sector?
options: Makes Indian MSME exports more expensive in global markets
Increases the cost of importing raw materials, potentially squeezing margins
Reduces the competitiveness of MSME products in international markets
Leads to a mandatory decrease in the production volume of export-oriented units
questions.explanation: While depreciation makes exports cheaper, it increases the cost of imported inputs, which many MSMEs rely upon.

questions.question: The 'TReDS' (Trade Receivables Discounting System) platform is primarily designed to address which of the following challenges for MSMEs?
options: Lack of skilled manpower in technical roles
Delayed payments from buyers and liquidity shortages
High corporate tax rates for small enterprises
Difficulty in obtaining environmental clearances
questions.explanation: TReDS is an online platform to facilitate the discounting of trade receivables to solve the problem of delayed payments.

questions.question: In the context of Indian Industry, what is the primary objective of 'Production Linked Incentive' (PLI) schemes?
options: To provide direct grants for the purchase of land
To encourage domestic manufacturing based on incremental sales
To subsidize the electricity costs for all industrial units
To eliminate the need for private investment in capital goods
questions.explanation: PLI schemes provide incentives based on the increase in sales of goods manufactured in India.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the 'Priority Sector Lending' (PSL) mandate regarding MSMEs?
options: Banks are prohibited from lending to MSMEs to avoid NPAs
Banks must allocate a specific percentage of their Adjusted Net Bank Credit to MSMEs
Only public sector banks are required to provide loans to MSMEs
PSL mandates that MSMEs must provide 100% collateral for all loans
questions.explanation: PSL ensures that credit flows to sectors like agriculture and MSMEs that are often neglected by formal banking.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key characteristic of 'Udyam Registration' for MSMEs?
options: It is a mandatory license required to start any business
It is a self-declaration based registration process integrated with GST and PAN
It is a physical registration process requiring multiple government approvals
It is only applicable to enterprises with more than 500 employees
questions.explanation: Udyam is a simplified, paperless, and self-certification-based registration process.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of the 'Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises' (CGTMSE)?
options: To provide equity funding to startups in the tech sector
To provide collateral-free credit to micro and small enterprises
To manage the export-import documentation for small firms
To provide insurance against fire and natural disasters
questions.explanation: CGTMSE provides credit guarantees to lenders to encourage collateral-free lending to MSMEs.

questions.question: Which of the following factors acts as a 'supply-side constraint' to the growth of the MSME sector in India?
options: Low demand for indigenous products in the domestic market
High cost of formal credit and lack of technological upgradation
Excessive availability of cheap labor
Low inflation in the prices of raw materials
questions.explanation: Limited access to formal credit and outdated technology are classic supply-side barriers to growth.

questions.question: Under the 'Make in India' initiative, the focus on 'Industrial Corridors' is intended to achieve which of the following?
options: To restrict industrial growth to specific urban centers
To reduce the reliance on road transport for all logistics
To create integrated ecosystems with better connectivity and infrastructure
To eliminate the need for MSMEs in the supply chain
questions.explanation: Industrial corridors aim to create clusters with high-quality infrastructure to improve efficiency and logistics.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of the 'TReDS' (Trade Receivables Discounting System) platform for MSMEs?
options: To provide low-interest collateral-free loans from the RBI
To facilitate the auction of unpaid invoices to financiers for immediate liquidity
To provide equity funding for scaling up production capacity
To regulate the pricing of raw materials in the industrial sector
questions.explanation: TReDS addresses the issue of delayed payments by allowing MSMEs to discount their trade receivables.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the 'Champion' scheme launched by the Ministry of MSME?
options: A scheme to provide free land for setting up industrial parks
A program to promote MSMEs as champions of export and technology upgrades
A credit guarantee scheme to eliminate the need for all bank collateral
A subsidy program for the purchase of agricultural machinery
questions.explanation: The CHAMPIONS portal aims to facilitate grievance redressal and promote MSMEs as global champions.

questions.question: How does a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee generally impact the MSME sector?
options: It decreases the competitiveness of MSME exports but lowers raw material import costs
It increases the cost of imported intermediate inputs while making exports more competitive
It leads to a decrease in domestic demand due to lower inflation
It has no impact as MSMEs only operate in the domestic market
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes exports cheaper for foreigners but increases costs for MSMEs relying on imported components.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key feature of the 'Udyam Registration' portal?
options: It requires mandatory physical verification of the industrial unit
It is a permanent registration based on Aadhaar and self-declaration
It is only applicable to enterprises with an annual turnover above ₹100 crore
It is a registration specifically for the agricultural sector only
questions.explanation: Udyam is a permanent, paperless, and self-declaration based registration process for MSMEs.

questions.question: In the context of MSME financing, what is the primary purpose of the 'Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises' (CGTMSE)?
options: To provide direct grants to startups for research and development
To provide collateral-free credit by providing guarantees to lending banks
To regulate the interest rates charged by Non-Banking Financial Companies
To act as a central repository for all industrial patents
questions.explanation: CGTMSE reduces the risk for lenders, encouraging them to provide loans without collateral to small units.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most likely impact of an increase in the Repo Rate by the RBI on MSMEs?
options: Decrease in the cost of borrowing and increase in capital expenditure
Increase in borrowing costs, potentially leading to reduced investment and liquidity
Automatic increase in the government's subsidy for industrial power
Decrease in the inflation of raw materials regardless of credit costs

questions.question: The concept of 'Cluster Development' in the MSME sector is primarily intended to:
options: Prevent competition between small units in the same geographic area
Achieve economies of scale through shared infrastructure and common facility centers
Limit the number of MSMEs in a specific region to prevent pollution
Ensure that only one large firm manages all small units in a region

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of the 'ZED' (Zero Defect Zero Effect) certification?

Improving quality to eliminate defects and reducing environmental impact
Providing a tax holiday for the first ten years of operation
Ensuring that all workers are paid a fixed government-mandated salary
questions.explanation: ZED focuses on enhancing global competitiveness by ensuring quality (Zero Defect) and sustainability (Zero Effect).

questions.question: Which of the following factors is the most significant 'structural' bottleneck for the growth of MSMEs in India?
options: Lack of demand for products in the domestic market
Difficulty in accessing formal credit and high cost of capital
Excessive availability of skilled labor in rural areas
Strict government regulations on the number of employees

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary tool of monetary policy used by the RBI?
options: A) Reserve Deposit Ratio
B) Open Market Operations
C) Quantitative Easing

questions.question: The fiscal deficit in India in recent years has been primarily driven by...
options: A) Foreign Investment
B) Higher Government Spending
C) Increased Tax Revenue
D) Inflation Control

questions.question: What effect does a depreciating rupee typically have on exports?
options: A) Decrease
B) No Effect
C) Increase

questions.question: How does an increase in the 'Priority Sector Lending' (PSL) targets for the banking sector typically impact the MSME sector?

It increases the cost of borrowing for small industries due to higher risk

questions.question: What is the primary objective of the 'TReDS' (Trade Receivables Discounting System) platform in the context of MSMEs?

To facilitate the discounting of trade receivables to solve liquidity issues
To provide insurance against industrial accidents
To regulate the pricing of raw materials for micro-industries

Indian MSME exports become more price-competitive in the global market

questions.question: The 'Credit Guarantee Fund Trust for Micro and Small Enterprises' (CGTMSE) primarily aims to:

Offer collateral-free credit to micro and small enterprises

questions.question: In the context of industrial development, what is the primary purpose of 'Special Economic Zones' (SEZs)?

questions.question: Which of the following factors most directly contributes to the 'Missing Middle' phenomenon in the Indian industrial structure?

The total absence of micro-enterprises in the manufacturing sector
Strict government bans on the growth of medium-sized industries

Government mandate to merge all micro-enterprises into a single large corporation

questions.question: Under the 'Production Linked Incentive' (PLI) schemes, how is the incentive typically calculated for the industry?

As a percentage of incremental sales of goods manufactured in India

As a loan waiver based on the total investment in land

questions.question: The 'TReDS' (Trade Receivables Discounting System) platform is primarily designed to address which of the following issues?

Indian MSME exports become cheaper and more competitive globally

To ensure zero tax liability for the first five years of operation
To achieve zero import dependence for all raw materials
